sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on the Rare Fruit Council of Australia and WANATCA archives, Australian nurseries and state agriculture departments, and recognised horticultural references.

core.intro: Loquats are one of the easiest and most rewarding fruit trees you can plant in Australia: a handsome evergreen that flowers in autumn and ripens one of the first crops of the year, in late winter and spring, well ahead of almost everything else. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are. The notes below apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Loquats are sold as named grafted varieties and as cheaper seedlings, and the named types are worth the extra. Bessell Brown is the popular Australian selection: large fruit with a thick, bruise-resistant orange skin and firm golden flesh of mild, pleasant flavour, and it is hardy across a wide range of climates. Nagasakiwase is the pick of the Japanese types, the earliest to ripen, with deep orange flesh, a high flesh-to-seed ratio and a very sweet flavour. Other named varieties in Australian nurseries include Champagne (an old favourite with juicy, sweet white flesh), the very large-fruited Enormity and Herds Mammoth, the golden Honey Dew, and Sewells Prolific. The loquat is a close cousin of the apple and pear, all members of the rose family. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

sections.heading: One tree will fruit
sections.body: You only need a single loquat. They are self-fertile, so one tree will set a crop on its own with no separate pollinator, which makes them one of the simplest fruit trees to grow. That said, a second variety nearby lifts both the size of the crop and the quality of the fruit, because cross-pollination improves fruit set even on a self-fertile tree. If you have room for two, plant two different named varieties; if you have room for one, plant it and it will still fruit.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give a loquat full sun for the heaviest crop, though it will take part shade and still make a fine tree. It is not fussy about soil and grows on most garden soils, much like its relative the apple, but it does best in a deep soil that drains freely and does not dry out hard, and it dislikes waterlogging. A little shelter from strong wind helps a lot, because the tender skin of the fruit bruises easily and the stiff, serrated leaves can scar fruit that rubs against them in the wind. Because it is evergreen, a loquat doubles as a year-round screen or shade tree, so plant it where you want that permanent green. Container-grown trees can go in for most of the year as long as you keep the water up through that first summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Loquats are fairly drought-hardy once established, but they reward steady moisture, so water young trees through their first summers and give any bearing tree a deep soak through dry spells, especially while the fruit is filling over late autumn, winter and into spring. They are light feeders. An annual mulch of compost or well-rotted manure, topped up with a complete fertiliser as growth resumes, keeps them productive, and on poorer soils they respond well to a balanced NPK feed. Go easy on nitrogen, since too much pushes soft, leafy growth at the expense of fruit.

sections.heading: Thinning for size
sections.body: Thinning is the one job that turns a loquat from disappointing to excellent. Each flower cluster can set a large number of fruit, and if they are left alone the fruit stays small, seedy and scarcely worth picking. Soon after the fruit sets, thin each cluster hard, down to about three or four fruit, and remove whole clusters where the tree has over-set. This sizes up what remains, improves the ratio of flesh to seed, and curbs the loquat's tendency to bear heavily one year and lightly the next. On a small tree you can slip a paper bag over each thinned cluster, which also keeps fruit fly and birds off.

sections.heading: Pruning and tree size
sections.body: Left alone a loquat grows into a rounded tree of around five to six metres, but it takes pruning well and is easily held lower so you can net and pick it. Prune in early summer, right after harvest and before the autumn growth flush that carries the flowers, removing the spent fruit stalks and thinning crowded shoots to keep the tree open and compact. Train a young tree to a clear trunk with three or four main branches in an open vase. A grafted named tree usually starts bearing within three to five years, while a seedling takes longer and gives unpredictable fruit.

sections.heading: Harvest and handling
sections.body: Loquats ripen from late winter into spring, among the first tree fruits of the year. Pick them only when they are fully coloured and slightly soft, because they do not sweeten or ripen further once picked. Cut each fruit or small cluster from the tree with scissors or secateurs rather than pulling, since the tender skin bruises at a touch, and handle them gently. The fruit is fragile and keeps only a few days, a week or so in the fridge if it was still firm when picked, which is why you rarely see loquats in the shops. Eat them fresh off the tree, or turn a glut into jam or jelly. The aromatic, slightly apple-like flesh is the part to eat: discard the large brown seeds, which, like apple and apricot kernels, are not for eating.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted loquat rather than a cheap unnamed seedling. Loquats do not come true from seed, so seedlings give unpredictable fruit and take longer to bear, while a grafted named variety fruits sooner and to type. Ask what rootstock the tree is on: grafted on its own seedling roots a loquat makes a large tree, while grafted on quince it stays compact and bears earlier, which is the better choice for a small garden or a large pot. Check the label for the variety name. The in-stock table above lists the named loquats for sale now and their prices.

faqs.q: Why does my loquat flower in autumn and winter?
faqs.a: That is simply how loquats work, and it is what makes them special. Unlike most fruit trees, they bloom in autumn and early winter and carry their fruit through the cold months to ripen in late winter and spring. The trade-off is that a hard frost can damage the open flowers and tiny fruit, so in cold districts the crop, rather than the tree, is what you protect.

faqs.q: How long until a loquat tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted, named loquat usually begins bearing within three to five years, and one grafted on quince rootstock often starts a little sooner. A tree raised from seed takes longer and the fruit is unpredictable, which is the main reason to start with a named, grafted plant.

faqs.q: Can I grow a loquat in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, especially a variety grafted on quince rootstock, which stays naturally compact. Use a large pot and a free-draining mix, water through summer because pots dry out fast, feed regularly since nutrients wash out of containers, and thin the fruit so the tree carries a sensible load. A potted loquat suits a courtyard or balcony and can be moved to a frost-free spot while it is flowering.

Eat the flesh, not the seeds. The large brown seeds, like the kernels inside apple and apricot pips, contain compounds that release small amounts of cyanide, so they should not be eaten, although the sweet flesh around them is perfectly safe and delicious. Just spit out or discard the seeds as you eat.

WA.intro: Growing loquats in Western Australia. Loquats are an easy, long-established backyard tree in Perth and the South West, where mild, near frost-free winters suit their unusual autumn-to-winter flowering and bring on one of the earliest crops in the country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Perth and the South West have the mild, near frost-free winters that loquats want. Because the tree flowers in autumn and carries its fruit through winter, a frost-free or only lightly frosted site lets the blossom set a full crop, which is why coastal and suburban Perth gardens fruit so reliably. The tree itself is hardy, but in the cooler Hills and frost-prone inland pockets a sharp frost can nip the open flowers and small fruit, so a warm, sheltered, north-facing spot gives the best results there.

sections.heading: Where loquats grow in WA
sections.body: The loquat is a classic, easy backyard fruit right across the Perth metropolitan area, valued as much for its glossy evergreen foliage as for its fruit. You will find heavy-bearing trees through the suburbs and the Swan Valley, into the cooler Perth Hills where frost timing matters more, and down through the South West. It asks very little once established.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's mild winters bring loquats on early. In Perth and the South West the main crop ripens from late winter into spring, roughly August into October, among the first tree fruit of the year. Pick only when the fruit is fully orange and slightly soft, because it will not ripen further off the tree.

sections.heading: Pests in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly is the main problem for loquats in WA. Medfly is endemic right across the state, from Esperance to Carnarvon, and DPIRD lists loquat among its most highly susceptible hosts. Worse, because loquats ripen through the cool months when little else is fruiting, an unmanaged tree helps carry the medfly population over winter, so bait, cover or bag the fruit and clean up every fallen loquat. Birds are the other pest, so net the tree as the fruit colours.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Loquat is a permitted plant on the Western Australian Organism List, with no loquat-specific quarantine restriction, so it is easier to source here than the many fruit trees WA's biosecurity rules keep out. Live plants from interstate must still meet WA's general plant-import conditions, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can legally ship to WA, alongside WA-grown stock.

faqs.q: Which loquat varieties suit Perth and the South West?
faqs.a: Perth's mild winters grow almost any loquat well. Bessell Brown is a dependable, bruise-resistant local favourite, Nagasakiwase ripens earliest and is very sweet, and the old Champagne is prized for its juicy flesh. Any of them will fruit happily in a frost-free Perth garden.

faqs.q: Do loquats cope with Perth's sandy soils?
faqs.a: Yes, loquats handle free-draining Perth sand better than many fruit trees. Dig in compost when planting, mulch thickly, and use a soil wetting agent if the sand has turned water-repellent. Give the tree a deep soak through the dry months and it rewards you with larger, sweeter fruit.

QLD.intro: Growing loquats in Queensland. Loquats need a cool season to flower and fruit well, so in Queensland they are a tree for the cooler, drier south and the uplands, and grow mostly as a handsome ornamental in the humid tropical lowlands.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Loquats are adapted to a subtropical to mild-temperate climate, and they crop well through subtropical south-east Queensland and the cooler southern uplands. In the warm, humid tropical lowlands the picture flips: the tree grows lushly but fruits poorly or not at all, because where the climate is too warm and moist a loquat is grown as an ornamental and will not bear well. For reliable fruit in Queensland, the cooler and drier the winter, the better.

sections.heading: Where loquats grow in QLD
sections.body: Loquats are a common, easy backyard tree across the cooler parts of the south-east, from Brisbane gardens out through the Lockyer Valley, and they fruit at their best in the cool, elevated Granite Belt around Stanthorpe and on the Darling Downs near Toowoomba, where winters are genuinely cold. The species grows so readily here that it has naturalised in parts of south-east Queensland.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland sees some of the earliest loquats in the country. The main crop ripens through late winter, around August and September in the warmer south-east, and in the mildest subtropical districts an early variety like Nagasakiwase can give a second, autumn crop, the first in April or May and the main one again in August. Pick when the fruit is fully coloured and slightly soft.

sections.heading: Pests in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the headline pest. It is native and endemic across eastern Australia, and loquat is on its host list, so ripe fruit will be stung unless it is covered, bagged or baited. Because loquats fruit through the cool months, a tree left unmanaged also helps carry fruit fly between seasons, so pick promptly and clear fallen fruit. Birds take ripe fruit too, so net the tree, and in humid weather the skin can blemish, though that is cosmetic and the flesh is fine.

faqs.q: Can I grow a loquat in tropical North Queensland?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree, but do not count on much fruit. In the warm, humid tropical lowlands a loquat grows vigorously as a glossy ornamental yet sets little fruit, because it needs a cooler winter to crop well. If you want loquats to eat in the tropics, your best chance is a cooler, elevated site rather than the humid coast.

faqs.q: Which loquat is best for a Queensland backyard?
faqs.a: In the subtropical south-east, Nagasakiwase is hard to beat: it ripens earliest, is very sweet, and can even give two crops a year in the warmest districts. Bessell Brown is the other safe pick, hardy and large-fruited, and in the cool Granite Belt almost any named variety will fruit well.

NSW.intro: Growing loquats in New South Wales. The loquat is one of the classic easy backyard trees of coastal and temperate NSW, especially around Sydney, ripening one of the first crops of the year while most other fruit trees are still bare.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Loquats suit the warm, temperate coast of NSW beautifully and crop reliably from the Sydney basin northward. The limit is frost rather than heat: because the tree flowers in autumn and carries fruit through winter, the coldest tablelands can lose blossom and small fruit to a hard frost, so up there a warm, sheltered, north-facing position makes the difference between a crop and none. On the milder coast, loquats need little more than a sunny spot.

sections.heading: Where loquats grow in NSW
sections.body: Old loquat trees shade backyards right across the Sydney basin and out along the Hawkesbury, up the Central Coast, and into the warm Northern Rivers, where the species grows so easily it has naturalised in places. They thin out only in the coldest inland and tableland districts, where frost on the winter blossom makes cropping less dependable.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: On the warm NSW coast loquats ripen early, from about August into October, with the warmest gardens picking first and the cooler, higher districts a few weeks later. As everywhere, pick the fruit only once it is fully coloured and slightly soft, because it will not ripen any further after picking.

sections.heading: Pests in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is the main concern. It is endemic along the coast and through many inland districts of NSW, and loquat is one of its hosts, so cover, bag or bait the fruit as it ripens. Loquats fruit in the cool season when other hosts are scarce, which makes a neglected tree a fruit fly reservoir, so pick promptly and clear fallen fruit. Net against birds as the fruit colours.

faqs.q: Why is the loquat such a common Sydney backyard tree?
faqs.a: It ticks every box for an easy garden tree: evergreen and good-looking year round, self-fertile so a single tree crops, tolerant of Sydney's soils and humidity, and it ripens fruit in late winter and spring when little else is ready. Many Sydney loquats are decades-old trees that fruit heavily with almost no care.

faqs.q: Are loquats a weed risk in New South Wales?
faqs.a: It is worth taking seriously on the warm coast. Birds spread the seed, and loquats have naturalised in parts of north-eastern NSW, so net or pick up fallen fruit, avoid planting right beside bushland reserves, and check your local council's advice in semi-rural areas.

VIC.intro: Growing loquats in Victoria. Loquats are hardy and widely grown across Melbourne and milder Victoria, and they ripen latest of the mainland states because the cool season slows the fruit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The loquat tree is genuinely hardy and shrugs off Victorian winters, which is why it is such a common sight in Melbourne gardens. The catch is its timing: because it flowers and sets fruit through autumn and winter, a hard frost can cut the crop even though the tree is untouched. Milder, coastal and bayside Melbourne gardens and warm, sheltered, north-facing spots fruit most reliably, while cold frost-hollow districts inland are more marginal for a dependable crop.

sections.heading: Where loquats grow in VIC
sections.body: Loquats grow happily through Melbourne's suburbs and the milder districts around the bay, out onto the Mornington Peninsula, and east through Gippsland, with scattered trees in the warmer north around the Goulburn Valley. The further you move into cold, frost-prone country, the more the autumn-to-winter blossom is at risk, so siting matters more in the cool interior than on the milder coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria ripens loquats latest of the mainland states. The cool season slows the fruit, so the main crop comes in spring, roughly September into November, rather than the late winter of the warmer states. Pick the fruit only when it is fully coloured and gives slightly, since it will not improve once it is off the tree.

sections.heading: Pests in VIC
sections.body: Birds are the main problem for loquats across Victoria, so net the tree as the fruit colours. Fruit fly pressure is lower than in the warm states but no longer absent: Queensland fruit fly has established in Victoria's warmer north, so gardeners there should cover or bag fruit and clear windfalls, while the cooler south stays comparatively low-risk. Diseases are rarely a serious issue in Victoria's drier air.

faqs.q: Will a loquat fruit in Melbourne's cool weather?
faqs.a: Yes. Loquats are widely grown and fruit across Melbourne, the cool season simply ripens the crop later, into spring. Give the tree a warm, sunny, north-facing position with some shelter from hard frost while it is flowering, and it will crop reliably.

faqs.q: Which loquat variety is best for a Victorian garden?
faqs.a: Choose a hardy, dependable type and give it a warm spot. Bessell Brown is a tough, large-fruited all-rounder, and Nagasakiwase ripens earliest and sweetest, which helps in a cool Melbourne spring. Plant against a north-facing wall or fence for extra warmth and the most reliable fruit.
